writer_persona: a showrunner for character-driven, relationship-first serial drama in the vein of prestige ensemble TV, specializing in family businesses under quiet financial and emotional siege
style_guidelines: Every scene serves at least one relationship, not just plot; withhold information the audience wants through character silence, not narrator exposition.
Keep dialogue naturalistic and clipped — people interrupt, deflect, and change the subject when a topic is too raw to face directly.
Let the Baltic setting (wind, gulls, cold light, off-season quiet) color mood without turning into travelogue description.
Root every accusation in genuine, defensible feeling — no character is simply 'the villain'; everyone believes they are doing the right thing.
Escalate through omission and mistiming (a call not returned, a letter not opened) rather than overt sabotage.

NARRATOR.default_style: calm, measured, a step removed from the action, warm but never sentimental
NARRATOR.description: the show's narrator — orients the listener at the start of every scene (who, where, when), never a character in the story

TOBIAS_VOSS.default_style: grounded, plainspoken, quick to warmth but quicker to defensiveness when the hotel is questioned
TOBIAS_VOSS.description: 34, Werner Voss's elder son from his first marriage; has run Hotel Wildrose's daily operations for six years and considers it the only home he's ever wanted; deeply loyal to his father's memory but has been avoiding the hotel's mail from the bank, and doesn't yet know how bad the finances really are.

LENA_VOSS.default_style: speaks slow, deliberate, very clear English, carrying a Shanghai-inflected cadence; controlled on the surface, restless underneath
LENA_VOSS.description: 31, Werner's daughter from his second marriage to Mei-Lin, who returned to China with Lena after the parents separated when Lena was twelve; now runs a small, struggling hospitality consultancy in Hamburg and needs capital fast; loves the hotel more than she admits and hides how much the possible sale costs her.

DAVID_LIN.default_style: smooth, persuasive, quick with a reassuring smile that doesn't always reach his eyes
DAVID_LIN.description: 35, acquisitions scout for a Chinese-owned coastal hospitality group; knew Lena in Shanghai years ago, a near-relationship that never quite happened; under pressure from his employer to close the Wildrose deal fast, and has been softening what his company actually intends to do with the property.

JULIA_WEN.default_style: warm, quick, self-deprecating humor that covers how much she's actually holding in
JULIA_WEN.description: 29, runs the Wildrose front desk and has worked there since she was nineteen; her family has run the harbor's only Chinese restaurant for two generations, and she grew up trilingual between the two businesses; has unspoken feelings for Tobias and is quietly terrified of what a sale would mean for her job and for him.

LOBBY.name: The Wildrose Lobby
LOBBY.description: A warm, wood-panelled reception hall with a worn brass bell on the counter, faded black-and-white photographs of three hotel generations lining the wall, and tall windows framing the grey harbor light.

TERRACE.name: The Sea-View Terrace
TERRACE.description: A weathered wooden deck jutting toward the Baltic, striped windbreaks snapping in the wind, mismatched deck chairs, and an open view of gulls wheeling over the pier.

STUDY.name: Werner's Study
STUDY.description: A cramped, dust-heavy attic office crowded with nautical charts, a rolltop desk stuffed with unopened envelopes, and a single bare bulb throwing long shadows over stacked ledgers.

audio.default_style: Speak clearly and with dramatic weight

series_intro: Off-season, the Baltic wind never really stops. Hotel Wildrose has stood on this stretch of coast for three generations, and now it belongs, in equal and uneasy halves, to a brother who has never wanted anything else and a sister who left it behind once already. This is the story of what a house costs when two people love it for entirely different reasons — and what happens when neither of them says so out loud.
series_outro: By the time the season turns, Wildrose still stands — but nothing about who owns it, who's leaving, and who's been lying to protect the people they love has settled. Some debts get paid. Others just get inherited, along with the house.

episodes.theme: Werner Voss's will splits Hotel Wildrose evenly between his two children and requires unanimous consent to sell, forcing Tobias's assumption of business-as-usual to collide with Lena's quiet decision to sell her half — while David Lin, the investor courting that sale, turns out to already know Lena far better than anyone else realizes.
episodes.outro_note: That night, alone in his father's study, Tobias finally opens the stack of red-stamped letters from the bank — and realizes the hotel he's spent six years defending has been drowning in debt for longer than he ever knew.
sections: LOBBY — The morning after Werner's funeral, the family lawyer reads his will: the hotel split evenly between Tobias and Lena, with a clause requiring both signatures to sell. For the first time out loud, Lena admits she's already been approached about selling — the opening beat of the Hidden Debt and sibling-conflict thread.

STUDY — Tobias and Julia start sorting Werner's papers, brushing past a stack of unopened bank envelopes; an unspoken current runs beneath the boxes and old photographs — Julia's Silence thread begins.

TERRACE — Lena meets David on the boardwalk to talk numbers, and their old familiarity is obvious before either names it; David rounds the offer up, softer than the truth — The Old Flame thread begins.

case.label: The Hidden Debt
case.solution: In his final three years, Werner secretly took out two loans against the hotel building to cover a botched renovation of the east wing and rising heating costs, and never told either child the real numbers; Lena learned the true scale of the debt through David's due-diligence review of the estate's finances before the will was even read, and has been sitting on that knowledge rather than confronting Tobias directly.
objective_facts: Werner refinanced the hotel twice in his last three years, using the building itself as collateral.
The most recent bank correspondence, including a formal notice of an overdue payment, arrived at the hotel two weeks before Werner's death and was never opened.
David's firm obtained a full financial picture of the hotel during preliminary due diligence, before Lena told anyone she was considering an offer.

knows: He has managed the hotel's day-to-day bookings and staff for six years and believes the business is, at worst, breaking even.
He has seen bank envelopes arrive addressed first to his father and now to the estate, and has set them aside unopened.
hides: He hasn't opened the recent bank mail at all — not because he suspects what's in it, but because handling his father's finances still feels like something he isn't ready to do.
believes_falsely: He believes Lena's push to sell comes from indifference to the hotel rather than from any real financial pressure, since he has no idea the business is in serious debt.

knows: Through David's due-diligence documents, she has seen the real scale of the debt — enough that a sale is close to the only way to clear it without losing the building outright.
hides: She hasn't told Tobias the actual numbers yet, partly to choose her moment and partly because she isn't ready to explain how she came to see confidential estate documents before he did.
believes_falsely: She assumes Tobias, as the one running the hotel, must already have some sense of how bad things are, and that his resistance to selling is willful denial rather than genuine ignorance.

knows: His firm's due diligence surfaced the full extent of the hotel's debt before the will was even read, which is part of why the offer he's authorized to make is time-limited.
hides: He hasn't clarified to Lena that this financial information technically shouldn't have reached her ahead of her brother.
believes_falsely: He assumes Lena will tell Tobias the numbers on her own timeline and doesn't realize how much that silence is already costing her with her brother.

case.label: The Old Flame
case.solution: Lena and David were nearly involved in Shanghai eight years ago, before career pressures pulled them apart; now reconnected professionally, David has quietly undersold to Lena what his company actually intends for Wildrose — a full redevelopment into a branded boutique chain property, not the light renovation he's implied — because he needs this acquisition to close to save his own position after a previous deal collapsed under him.
objective_facts: Lena and David worked together at a hospitality group in Shanghai eight years ago and were, by David's own account, 'a near-miss.'
David's company's internal development plan for Wildrose calls for gutting most of the original building and rebranding it entirely.
David's last major acquisition for the firm fell through six months ago, and this deal was assigned to him as a probationary chance to prove himself.

knows: She and David have a real history from Shanghai, and reconnecting with him professionally has stirred feelings she hasn't examined.
hides: She hasn't told Tobias that she personally knows the buyer's representative, or how much that connection has shaped how much she trusts David's version of the offer.
believes_falsely: She believes David has told her the company's real plans for the building — a sympathetic, mostly-preserving renovation — because that's the story he's chosen to tell her.

knows: He knows exactly what his company intends to do to the building if the sale closes, and that it bears little resemblance to what he's described to Lena.
He knows this deal is his last real chance to prove himself to his employer after his previous acquisition collapsed.
hides: He has deliberately softened the redevelopment plan when talking to Lena, telling himself he'll find a way to protect the parts of the building that matter to her once the deal closes.
believes_falsely: He believes he can still negotiate better terms for preservation after the sale goes through, underestimating how little say he'll actually have once his firm owns the property outright.

case.label: Julia's Silence
case.solution: Julia has quietly interviewed for a front-desk manager position at a larger hotel in Rostock after overhearing enough about the possible sale to fear for her job, and has said nothing to Tobias — not only to protect him from one more thing to worry about, but because she doesn't want him to know how much his own uncertainty about the hotel has made her reconsider her future at Wildrose, feelings for him included.
objective_facts: Julia interviewed for a position at a hotel in Rostock four days before Werner's funeral.
Julia has worked at Wildrose since she was nineteen and has never worked anywhere else.
Neither Tobias nor Lena has said anything to Julia directly about the hotel's possible sale; she pieced it together from overheard conversations.

knows: He relies on Julia for nearly everything at the front desk and has for years, though he's never quite named what she means to him beyond that.
believes_falsely: He assumes Julia's steadiness means she isn't worried about the hotel's future, when in fact she's the one person most quietly bracing for it to end.

knows: She overheard enough of Lena and Tobias's conversations to guess a sale is being considered, well before either of them told her anything directly.
hides: She has told no one that she interviewed in Rostock, afraid that saying it out loud — to Tobias especially — would make it feel like she's already decided to leave.
believes_falsely: She assumes Tobias sees her as essential staff and nothing more, unaware of how much more he's actually noticed.

episodes.theme: Tobias confronts Lena over the debt he's just discovered and the buyer's rep she never mentioned knowing, David's company pushes him to close fast and forces him to admit more than he intended about Wildrose's real fate, and Julia's Rostock offer becomes real just as Tobias, for the first time, tries to tell her what she means to him.
episodes.intro_note: The unopened bank letters Tobias found the night before have confirmed what Lena already knew: Hotel Wildrose is carrying more debt than either of them can easily absorb — and Tobias still doesn't know how Lena found out first, or that she knows the buyer personally.
sections: STUDY — Tobias confronts Lena with the bank letters he found the night before, demanding to know what she knew and when — a short, sharp collision, no room to soften it. The Hidden Debt thread reaches its first real reckoning.
TERRACE — Lena presses David for what his company actually intends for the building, and gets more of an honest answer than he meant to give. The Old Flame thread turns.
LOBBY — Julia takes a callback from the Rostock hotel with a real offer in hand, and almost tells Tobias before she loses her nerve. Julia's Silence thread advances.

LOBBY (later) — That evening, with the day's damage still raw, Tobias finds Julia closing up the front desk and, haltingly, says more than he planned to. Julia's Silence thread turns toward the personal.

case.label: The Hidden Debt
case.solution: In his final three years, Werner secretly took out two loans against the hotel building to cover a botched renovation of the east wing and rising heating costs, and never told either child the real numbers; Lena learned the true scale of the debt through David's due-diligence review of the estate's finances before the will was even read, and has been sitting on that knowledge rather than confronting Tobias directly.
objective_facts: Werner refinanced the hotel twice in his last three years, using the building itself as collateral.
The most recent bank correspondence, including a formal notice of an overdue payment, arrived at the hotel two weeks before Werner's death and was never opened.
David's firm obtained a full financial picture of the hotel during preliminary due diligence, before Lena told anyone she was considering an offer.

knows: He has now read the bank letters himself and knows the hotel carries two loans against the building, with an overdue payment on the most recent one.
He knows Lena had access to the full financial picture before he did, through David's due diligence.
hides: He hides how frightened the real numbers make him behind an accusation that Lena orchestrated all of this from the start.
believes_falsely: He suspects Lena arranged for David's firm to get the estate's financials early on purpose, when in fact she was simply forwarded documents she hadn't asked for and didn't know how to hand back.

knows: She now knows Tobias has seen the real numbers and that the confrontation she's been avoiding is no longer avoidable.
hides: She still hasn't told him that David is the one who personally walked her through the numbers, or how far back she and David actually go.
believes_falsely: She reads Tobias's anger as him blaming her for the debt itself, rather than for keeping it from him — missing that what actually wounds him is being kept in the dark, not the debt.

knows: His firm's due diligence surfaced the full extent of the hotel's debt before the will was even read, which is part of why the offer he's authorized to make is time-limited.
He knows his firm is pressuring him to get signatures this week, before either sibling has time to fully absorb the financial picture.
hides: He hasn't clarified to Lena that this financial information technically shouldn't have reached her ahead of her brother.
believes_falsely: He assumes Lena will tell Tobias the numbers on her own timeline and doesn't realize how much that silence is already costing her with her brother.

case.label: The Old Flame
case.solution: Lena and David were nearly involved in Shanghai eight years ago, before career pressures pulled them apart; now reconnected professionally, David has quietly undersold to Lena what his company actually intends for Wildrose — a full redevelopment into a branded boutique chain property, not the light renovation he's implied — because he needs this acquisition to close to save his own position after a previous deal collapsed under him.
objective_facts: Lena and David worked together at a hospitality group in Shanghai eight years ago and were, by David's own account, 'a near-miss.'
David's company's internal development plan for Wildrose calls for gutting most of the original building and rebranding it entirely.
David's last major acquisition for the firm fell through six months ago, and this deal was assigned to him as a probationary chance to prove himself.

knows: She and David have a real history from Shanghai, and reconnecting with him professionally has stirred feelings she hasn't examined.
She now knows, after pressing him directly, that David's company intends to gut most of the original building rather than preserve it as he first implied.
hides: She still hasn't told Tobias that she personally knows the buyer's representative, or how much that connection has shaped how much she trusts David's version of the offer.
believes_falsely: She assumes that David finally telling her the truth about the redevelopment plan means he's being fully straight with her now, not realizing how little control he'll actually have to soften the outcome even if he wanted to.

knows: He knows exactly what his company intends to do to the building if the sale closes, and that it bears little resemblance to what he first described to Lena.
He knows admitting the redevelopment plan to Lena directly jeopardizes both the deal and his own position, but couldn't keep lying to her face when she asked outright.
hides: He still hasn't told her that his own job depends on closing this specific deal within his employer's timeline, which would make his motives look far more mercenary than he'd like her to think.
believes_falsely: He believes he can still negotiate better terms for preservation after the sale goes through, underestimating how little say he'll actually have once his firm owns the property outright.

case.label: Julia's Silence
case.solution: Julia has quietly interviewed for a front-desk manager position at a larger hotel in Rostock after overhearing enough about the possible sale to fear for her job, and has said nothing to Tobias — not only to protect him from one more thing to worry about, but because she doesn't want him to know how much his own uncertainty about the hotel has made her reconsider her future at Wildrose, feelings for him included.
objective_facts: Julia interviewed for a position at a hotel in Rostock four days before Werner's funeral.
Julia has worked at Wildrose since she was nineteen and has never worked anywhere else.
Neither Tobias nor Lena has said anything to Julia directly about the hotel's possible sale; she pieced it together from overheard conversations.

knows: He relies on Julia for nearly everything at the front desk and has for years, though he's never quite named what she means to him beyond that.
He now realizes, watching her hesitate over a phone call, that something is weighing on her she hasn't told him.
hides: He hasn't told her yet how much of his own certainty about keeping the hotel has cracked since finding the bank letters.
believes_falsely: He assumes that if the hotel really were in danger of falling apart, Julia would simply tell him outright, not realizing how much she's protecting him from exactly that.

knows: She overheard enough of Lena and Tobias's conversations to guess a sale is being considered, well before either of them told her anything directly.
She now has a real offer from the Rostock hotel, with a start date that would force a decision within the month.
hides: She has told no one that the Rostock offer is now real and not hypothetical, afraid that saying it out loud — to Tobias especially — would make it feel like she's already decided to leave.
believes_falsely: She assumes Tobias sees her as essential staff and nothing more, unaware of how much more he's actually noticed.
